stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman attends antenatal clinic at 28+3 weeks for routine review. Her booking ferritin was 18 mcg/L and she was started on oral iron polymaltose 100 mg daily at 16 weeks. She has taken it consistently but feels increasingly fatigued and short of breath climbing one flight of stairs. There is no history of menorrhagia, no current GI bleeding, no antepartum bleeding, and her diet includes red meat twice weekly.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, fundal height 28 cm, foetal heart 142, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 11 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 9%, CRP 3 mg/L, platelets 268 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue oral iron polymaltose and recheck full blood count in six weeks
options.rationale: She has had over 12 weeks of oral iron without an adequate Hb response and ferritin remains low; continuing the same agent risks an unprepared third trimester. (Source: RANZCOG iron in pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Switch to oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily plus ascorbic acid
options.rationale: An oral switch is reasonable earlier in pregnancy but with Hb 92 g/L at 28 weeks and documented oral failure, the gain in Hb over 6 to 8 weeks is unlikely to reach target before delivery. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg intravenously as a day-case infusion
options.rationale: Hb under 100 g/L after second trimester with failed oral iron and 12 weeks to term meets RANZCOG criteria for parenteral iron; ferric carboxymaltose is the preferred AU agent. (Source: RANZCOG iron in pregnancy 2023; AMH ferric carboxymaltose)

options.text: Cross-match and transfuse two units of packed red blood cells overnight
options.rationale: Transfusion in stable antenatal anaemia is reserved for Hb under 70 g/L or symptomatic instability; this patient is haemodynamically stable with a treatable cause. (Source: NBA patient blood management module 5)

options.text: Commence subcutaneous erythropoietin alfa weekly and recheck reticulocytes
options.rationale: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not first-line for uncomplicated iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy; iron replacement alone restores erythropoiesis. (Source: AMH erythropoietins)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia complicates 15 to 25 percent of Australian pregnancies. RANZCOG endorses parenteral iron when oral iron has failed (no Hb rise after 2 to 4 weeks of adherent therapy), when there is malabsorption or intolerance, or when there is insufficient time to delivery to correct anaemia orally. Ferric carboxymaltose can deliver 1000 mg in a single infusion and is the AU first-line parenteral preparation. Antenatal correction reduces postpartum transfusion risk and improves neonatal iron stores.
key_points: Treat antenatal Hb under 110 g/L (first trimester) or under 105 g/L (second/third) plus ferritin under 30 mcg/L.
Oral iron failure = no Hb rise over 2 to 4 weeks of adherent dosing.
Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single-dose IV is AU first-line parenteral.
Transfusion threshold in stable antenatal anaemia is Hb under 70 g/L.
Aim for Hb >= 110 g/L by term to reduce postpartum transfusion risk.
explanation.pearls: Ferric carboxymaltose causes transient hypophosphataemia in up to 50 percent of recipients; check serum phosphate at 2 weeks if symptoms of bone pain or fatigue persist.

subtopic_detail: Group B Streptococcus intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman is admitted in established labour at 39+1 weeks. Her vaginal-rectal swab at 36 weeks isolated Group B Streptococcus and she has no known antibiotic allergies. She is fully informed about the screening result and consents to recommended treatment. Membranes ruptured 1 hour ago with clear liquor; cervix is 5 cm dilated and contracting 4 in 10 minutes.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 124/76, HR 88, T 36.9°C; CTG baseline 140, accelerations present, no decelerations
data_table.Examination: Vertex at -1 station, intact perineum, no chorioamnionitis features
data_table.Investigations: 36-week swab: GBS positive (no isolate sensitivities reported)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Amoxicillin 2 g orally as a single loading dose then 1 g every 6 hours
options.rationale: Oral amoxicillin does not achieve reliable amniotic-fluid concentrations during active labour; AU guidance requires intravenous therapy. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 3 g intravenously then 1.8 g every 4 hours until delivery
options.rationale: AU intrapartum prophylaxis for GBS-positive women without penicillin allergy is benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours, aiming for at least one dose at least 4 hours before delivery. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in pregnancy 2024; eTG)

options.text: Clindamycin 600 mg intravenously every 8 hours until delivery is complete
options.rationale: Clindamycin is the alternative only for severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy with documented isolate susceptibility; this patient has no allergy. (Source: RANZCOG GBS 2024)

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g intravenously then 1 g every 8 hours throughout active labour
options.rationale: Cefazolin is appropriate for non-severe penicillin allergy; with no allergy benzylpenicillin remains preferred for narrower spectrum. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Vancomycin 20 mg/kg intravenously over 2 hours then redose at 12 hours
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin-resistant isolate; it is not first-line in an unallergic patient. (Source: RANZCOG GBS 2024)

explanation.summary: Australian intrapartum GBS prophylaxis uses universal antenatal swabbing at 35 to 37 weeks (RANZCOG 2024) followed by IV antibiotics in labour for swab-positive women, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, a previous GBS-affected infant, or unknown status with risk factors (preterm, ROM >= 18 hours, intrapartum fever). Benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours is first-line; at least one dose 4 hours pre-delivery is the adequate-prophylaxis benchmark. Alternatives are stratified by allergy severity and isolate susceptibility.
key_points: Universal swab at 35 to 37 weeks (AU 2024 RANZCOG change from risk-based).
First-line: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load, then 1.8 g every 4 hours.
Non-severe penicillin allergy: cefazolin 2 g IV load, then 1 g every 8 hours.
Severe (anaphylaxis) penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600 mg IV every 8 hours if isolate susceptible; otherwise vancomycin.
Adequate prophylaxis = at least one dose at least 4 hours before delivery.
explanation.pearls: Inadequate intrapartum prophylaxis (less than 4 hours before delivery) does not change neonatal observation: well infants of GBS-positive mothers still need 24 hours of structured observation per NeoRes guidance.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Early-onset GBS disease 2024

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman presents at 38+5 weeks reporting a gush of clear fluid 4 hours ago with continuing leakage. Her previous pregnancy ended with a spontaneous vaginal birth at 39 weeks; this pregnancy has been uncomplicated and her 36-week GBS swab was negative. She is afebrile, the foetus is moving, and she is not contracting. Speculum confirms clear liquor pooling in the posterior fornix with positive amnisure.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 38 cm, longitudinal lie, cephalic, FHR 140, no uterine tenderness; speculum clear liquor, cervix appears closed visually
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab 36 weeks negative; CTG reactive; group B and D Rh-positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan of care?

options.text: Discharge home with daily review and induction if not in labour by 96 hours
options.rationale: Australian practice is inpatient observation or expedited induction after term PROM; 96 hours of expectant management increases chorioamnionitis risk without clear benefit. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2023)

options.text: Commence prophylactic intravenous benzylpenicillin and expectant management to 48 hours
options.rationale: GBS-negative women do not need empirical antibiotics in the absence of chorioamnionitis features; antibiotics do not reduce neonatal sepsis without infection. (Source: RANZCOG GBS in pregnancy 2024)

options.text: Perform prostaglandin gel insertion immediately to ripen the cervix further first
options.rationale: PGE2 in the presence of ruptured membranes increases chorioamnionitis risk and oxytocin is preferred when the cervix is favourable. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour

options.text: Offer induction of labour with oxytocin infusion within 24 hours of rupture
options.rationale: TERMPROM and AU guidance favour induction within 24 hours of term PROM to reduce maternal and neonatal infection; oxytocin is first-line with a favourable cervix and no contraindication. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2023)

options.text: Recommend an immediate elective caesarean section due to ruptured membranes risk
options.rationale: Term PROM is not an indication for caesarean; vaginal birth is associated with better maternal and neonatal outcomes when there is no obstetric contraindication. (Source: RANZCOG term PROM 2023)

explanation.summary: Term prelabour rupture of membranes (PROM, after 37 weeks) occurs in about 8 percent of pregnancies. The TERMPROM trial and subsequent AU guidance show that immediate induction (within 24 hours) reduces chorioamnionitis and early neonatal sepsis without increasing caesarean rates compared with expectant management. Oxytocin is the AU first-line agent when the cervix is favourable; prostaglandins are reserved for unfavourable cervix and carry a higher infection risk after ROM. GBS prophylaxis is needed only for positive carriers, prior GBS-affected infant, or chorioamnionitis features.
key_points: Term PROM = ROM after 37 weeks before labour; speculum + amnisure or pooling for diagnosis.
Offer induction within 24 hours of ROM to reduce infection.
Oxytocin first-line if favourable cervix; PG agents increase infection risk post-ROM.
GBS-negative women do not need prophylactic antibiotics in absence of clinical chorio.
Maternal fever, foetal tachycardia, foul liquor, or uterine tenderness = treat as chorioamnionitis.
explanation.pearls: After term PROM, neonatal early-onset sepsis risk rises sharply after 18 hours of ruptured membranes; document the rupture time precisely as it triggers neonatal observation pathways.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour

stem: A 31-year-old woman 3 weeks postpartum following an uncomplicated vaginal birth presents with 36 hours of right-sided breast pain, redness and a flu-like illness. She has been exclusively breastfeeding her infant but feeding on the affected side has become very painful. She has no penicillin allergy and is otherwise well. She wants to continue breastfeeding if possible.

data_table.Examination: Right upper outer breast: 6 cm wedge-shaped erythema, warm and tender, no fluctuance, no nipple cracks; left breast normal; infant suckling well
data_table.Investigations: Not performed; routine bloods not indicated at this stage

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Cease breastfeeding from the right breast and pump until the redness resolves completely
options.rationale: Continued effective milk removal from the affected breast accelerates resolution; abrupt cessation worsens engorgement and abscess risk. (Source: ABA mastitis 2023)

options.text: Arrange urgent ultrasound and incision and drainage of the breast under anaesthetic
options.rationale: There is no fluctuance on examination; imaging-drainage is reserved for suspected or confirmed abscess. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Switch to oral cephalexin 500 mg every 6 hours and warm compresses three times daily
options.rationale: Cephalexin is a reasonable alternative only in non-severe penicillin allergy; with no allergy flucloxacillin gives narrower S. aureus cover. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Trial 24 hours of cabbage leaves, ibuprofen and effective milk removal with no antibiotics
options.rationale: Conservative measures are appropriate within the first 24 hours; persistent systemic features beyond 24 hours indicate bacterial mastitis requiring antibiotics. (Source: ABA mastitis 2023)

options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily plus continued breastfeeding
options.rationale: Inflammatory mastitis with systemic features after 24 hours warrants empirical anti-staphylococcal therapy; flucloxacillin is AU first-line and breastfeeding should continue. (Source: eTG; ABA mastitis 2023)

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis spans an inflammatory continuum from ductal narrowing through bacterial infection (most commonly Staphylococcus aureus) to abscess. The 2022 ABM and 2023 ABA updates emphasise that effective milk removal, anti-inflammatories and cool compresses are first-line; antibiotics are added when systemic features persist beyond 24 hours, nipple trauma is present, or the patient is septic. Flucloxacillin is AU first-line antibiotic. Cessation of feeding on the affected side worsens engorgement and increases abscess risk.
key_points: Continue feeding from the affected breast; effective drainage is therapeutic.
Add antibiotics if systemic features beyond 24 hours or nipple trauma.
Flucloxacillin 500 mg QID PO is AU first-line.
Cephalexin is an alternative for non-severe penicillin allergy.
Persistent symptoms after 48 hours of antibiotics warrant ultrasound to exclude abscess.
explanation.pearls: An unresolving 'mastitis' in a non-lactating or peri-weaning woman over 35 warrants ultrasound and biopsy to exclude inflammatory breast cancer, regardless of antibiotic response.

sources.label: Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine Protocol 36: Mastitis Spectrum 2022

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 woman attends for her 26-week OGTT. Her previous pregnancy was uncomplicated with a 3400 g infant born at 39 weeks. Pre-pregnancy BMI is 24.5; she has no family history of diabetes. Her booking HbA1c was 36 mmol/mol. She has been well throughout this pregnancy and the morphology scan was normal. She is anxious about the result and wants to know what happens next.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 26 cm, longitudinal lie, FHR 144; no peripheral oedema

lead_in: Which step most appropriately progresses her management?

options.text: Reassure her that all values are within the normal range for pregnancy
options.rationale: Fasting glucose of 5.4 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS 2014 cut-off of 5.1 mmol/L, so she meets criteria for GDM. (Source: ADIPS 2014)

options.text: Repeat the OGTT in 4 weeks with the same 75 g loading dose for confirmation
options.rationale: A single OGTT meeting ADIPS criteria is diagnostic; repeating delays treatment and increases macrosomia risk. (Source: ADIPS 2014)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily and arrange dietitian review within two weeks
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is only added after a 1 to 2 week trial of lifestyle measures fails to achieve targets; immediate metformin is not first-line. (Source: NDSS GDM management 2023)

options.text: Refer urgently to endocrinology for insulin commencement before the next antenatal visit
options.rationale: Insulin is reserved for failure of lifestyle and metformin; with no maternal hyperglycaemic symptoms and modestly elevated values, insulin first-up is inappropriate. (Source: NDSS GDM management 2023)

options.text: Diagnose gestational diabetes and refer for diabetes educator and home BSL monitoring
options.rationale: Fasting at or above 5.1 mmol/L meets ADIPS GDM criteria; first-line management is dietitian, education and four-times-daily BSL monitoring before considering pharmacotherapy. (Source: ADIPS 2014; NDSS GDM management)

explanation.summary: ADIPS 2014 criteria diagnose GDM on a 75 g OGTT if any of fasting >= 5.1, 1 hour >= 10.0 or 2 hour >= 8.5 mmol/L. A single elevated value is diagnostic; repeat testing is not required. Initial management is diabetes-educator-led, with dietitian input and home BSL monitoring (fasting plus 1 or 2 hour postprandial). Pharmacotherapy is added after 1 to 2 weeks of lifestyle measures fail to keep fasting <= 5.0 and postprandial <= 6.7 (1 hour) or <= 6.0 (2 hour). Metformin is AU first-line for women without contraindications; insulin is added or substituted for failure.
key_points: ADIPS cut-offs: fasting 5.1, 1 hour 10.0, 2 hour 8.5 mmol/L (any one is diagnostic).
Single elevated value is diagnostic - do not repeat.
Initial Tx: educator + dietitian + home BSL monitoring.
Targets: fasting <= 5.0, 1 hr postprandial <= 7.4, 2 hr postprandial <= 6.7.
Pharmacotherapy if targets unmet at 1 to 2 weeks: metformin first, insulin as needed.
explanation.pearls: Women diagnosed with GDM have a 50 percent risk of developing type 2 diabetes within 10 years; book a postpartum 75 g OGTT at 6 to 12 weeks and annual fasting glucose or HbA1c thereafter.
